summary: Bordeaux is France's largest fine-wine region, famed for age-worthy red blends built on Cabernet Sauvignon (Left Bank) and Merlot (Right Bank), alongside Cabernet Franc and Petit Verdot. It also produces dry whites from Sauvignon Blanc and Sémillon and world-renowned botrytized sweet wines from Sauternes and Barsac. Styles range from structured, tannic Médoc reds to plush Pomerol Merlots and honeyed dessert wines.
classification: Several systems coexist. The 1855 Classification ranks Médoc (plus Haut-Brion in Graves) reds into five growths (First through Fifth Growths) and Sauternes/Barsac sweet wines into Premier Cru Supérieur (Yquem alone), First and Second Growths. Graves/Pessac-Léognan has its own Crus Classés de Graves. Saint-Émilion is reclassified roughly every decade (most recently 2022) into Premier Grand Cru Classé A, Premier Grand Cru Classé B, and Grand Cru Classé. Pomerol has no official classification.

producers.name: Château Latour

summary: Southwest France is a diverse patchwork of appellations stretching from the Dordogne to the Pyrenees, prized for distinctive indigenous grapes such as Malbec (Cahors), Tannat (Madiran) and Petit/Gros Manseng (Jurançon). It produces deep, structured reds, honeyed sweet whites, crisp dry whites and traditional sparkling wines, often at excellent value. Many Bergerac-area wines echo Bordeaux blends, while the deep south offers unique styles.
classification: Unlike Bordeaux, the Southwest has no growth hierarchy; quality is organized through its many AOC/AOP appellations: Cahors (Malbec), Madiran (Tannat) with sweet white Pacherenc du Vic-Bilh, Bergerac and Monbazillac (Bordeaux-style reds and botrytized sweet whites), Jurançon (Manseng), Gaillac (diverse), Fronton (Négrette), Marcillac (Fer Servadou), Saint-Mont, and Basque Irouléguy.

producers.name: Clos Triguedina
